tasks.title: Inspect CoreDNS and a Pod's resolv.conf
tasks.prompt:
Without leaving your terminal, confirm that CoreDNS is running in the cluster and that a freshly launched Pod is wired to it.

1. List the `kube-dns` Service in `kube-system` and note its ClusterIP.
2. List the CoreDNS Pods and their node assignments.
3. Launch a throwaway Pod using the `agnhost` image and print its `/etc/resolv.conf`. Confirm that the `nameserver` line matches the ClusterIP you found in step 1, and record the `search` suffixes and the `ndots` value.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1 – DNS Service
kubectl -n kube-system get svc kube-dns

# Step 2 – CoreDNS Pods
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=kube-dns -o wide

# Step 3 – Launch a throwaway Pod and inspect resolv.conf
kubectl run dnsutils --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=registry.k8s.io/e2e-test-images/agnhost:2.39 -- /bin/sh
# Inside the Pod:
# cat /etc/resolv.conf
tasks.solution:
# Expected output of step 1 (ClusterIP is typically 10.96.0.10):
# NAME       TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)         AGE
# kube-dns   ClusterIP   10.96.0.10    <none>        53/UDP,53/TCP   ...

# Expected output of step 2:
# NAME                       READY   STATUS    NODE
# coredns-<hash>-<id1>       1/1     Running   <node-name>
# coredns-<hash>-<id2>       1/1     Running   <node-name>

# Expected /etc/resolv.conf inside the Pod (namespace = default):
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
# options ndots:5
tasks.explanation: kubelet writes `/etc/resolv.conf` for every Pod at startup. The `nameserver` is the ClusterIP of the `kube-dns` Service, which load-balances across the two CoreDNS Pods. The `search` suffixes let short names like `kubernetes` expand automatically to `kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local`. The `ndots:5` option means any name with fewer than 5 dots is treated as unqualified and the search list is tried first — which is why a single-word name resolves inside the cluster without typing the full FQDN.
hints: If the `agnhost` image is not available in your registry, any image that includes `nslookup`/`cat` works (e.g., `busybox`, `nicolaka/netshoot`). With busybox: `kubectl run dnsutils --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox -- sh`.

The ClusterIP of `kube-dns` is set when kubeadm (or your installer) bootstraps the cluster. It may differ from `10.96.0.10` if a custom `--service-cidr` was used — always read it from `kubectl get svc`, do not assume.
If you see three or more search domains (e.g. `default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local`), that is normal: kubelet adds the Pod's own namespace suffix first, then the broader ones.

tasks.title: Resolve Services with nslookup and dig (short name, FQDN, cross-namespace)
tasks.prompt:
From inside the throwaway Pod (`agnhost` or `busybox`), resolve Services three different ways to understand how the search-domain expansion works.

1. Resolve the built-in `kubernetes` Service by its **short name**: `nslookup kubernetes`. Note the server address and the resolved IP.
2. Resolve the same Service by its **full FQDN**: `nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local`. Confirm both return the same IP.
3. Resolve the `kube-dns` Service which lives in `kube-system` — a **different namespace** — using its cross-namespace short form: `nslookup kube-dns.kube-system`.
4. Use `dig` to do a **reverse (PTR) lookup** on the IP you got in step 1: `dig -x <ClusterIP> +short`.

For each lookup, record what the resolver sends to CoreDNS (use `dig +search` to see the expanded name).
tasks.starter:
# Inside the throwaway Pod shell:

# 1. Short name
nslookup kubernetes

# 2. FQDN
nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local

# 3. Cross-namespace short form
nslookup kube-dns.kube-system

# 4. Reverse lookup (replace IP with the actual ClusterIP from step 1)
dig -x 10.96.0.1 +short

# Bonus – see the expanded query name
dig +search kubernetes +short
tasks.solution:
# 1. Short-name lookup – resolver appends search suffixes until one hits:
#    nslookup kubernetes
#    Server:   10.96.0.10
#    Address:  10.96.0.10#53
#    Name:     kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
#    Address:  10.96.0.1
#
# 2. FQDN lookup – same result, no search expansion needed:
#    nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
#    Name:     kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
#    Address:  10.96.0.1
#
# 3. Cross-namespace – two-label name still has fewer than 5 dots,
#    so search expansion runs; kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local resolves:
#    Name:     kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local
#    Address:  10.96.0.10
#
# 4. Reverse (PTR) lookup:
#    dig -x 10.96.0.1 +short
#    kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local.
tasks.explanation: This exercise traces the full resolution path: short name → search-domain expansion → CoreDNS → A record. Step 1 vs step 2 shows that both reach the same record; the difference is purely in how many DNS queries are made before CoreDNS is hit. Step 3 confirms that cross-namespace lookups work as long as you include the namespace label: `kube-dns.kube-system` has 1 dot (< ndots 5), so the search list turns it into `kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local` automatically. Step 4 demonstrates PTR records, which CoreDNS synthesizes for Service ClusterIPs in the `in-addr.arpa` zone.
hints: If `dig` is not available, `nslookup -type=PTR <IP>` gives the same PTR record. With busybox, `nslookup <IP>` also issues a PTR query by default.
If the lookup of `kube-dns.kube-system` fails, check that you are in the Pod shell (not on the host) — the host's resolv.conf does not have the cluster search domains.
To watch the search expansion live, run `dig +search +showsearch kubernetes` — it prints each attempted name before the final answer.

tasks.title: Create a headless Service and observe per-Pod A records and SRV records
tasks.prompt:
Normal Services hide Pods behind one ClusterIP. A headless Service returns individual Pod IPs. Prove this difference with a real Deployment.

1. Create a Deployment `db-app` (2 replicas, image `nginx:alpine`, label `app: db`) in namespace `default`.
2. Create a **normal** (ClusterIP) Service named `db-normal` targeting `app: db` on port 80.
3. Create a **headless** Service named `db-headless` targeting the same Pods on port 80 with a named port `http`.
4. From inside a throwaway Pod, run `dig +short db-normal.default.svc.cluster.local` and `dig +short db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local`. Record the number of IP addresses returned by each.
5. Query the SRV record for the headless Service: `dig +short SRV _http._tcp.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local`.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Deployment
kubectl create deployment db-app --image=nginx:alpine --replicas=2
kubectl label deployment db-app app=db --overwrite
# (or apply the YAML below)

# 2. Normal Service
kubectl expose deployment db-app --name=db-normal --port=80 --target-port=80

# 3. Headless Service – must use YAML (kubectl expose cannot set clusterIP: None)
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: db-headless
  namespace: default
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: db
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: 80
EOF

# 4. DNS queries from a throwaway Pod
kubectl run dnsutils --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=registry.k8s.io/e2e-test-images/agnhost:2.39 -- /bin/sh
# Inside:
# dig +short db-normal.default.svc.cluster.local
# dig +short db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local

# 5. SRV record
# dig +short SRV _http._tcp.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
tasks.solution:
# Step 4 results:
#
# dig +short db-normal.default.svc.cluster.local
# 10.108.x.x          <- one ClusterIP, regardless of replica count
#
# dig +short db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# 10.244.x.x          <- Pod IP 1 (actual pod IP, not a virtual IP)
# 10.244.y.y          <- Pod IP 2
# (order may vary; CoreDNS randomizes the set)
#
# Step 5 – SRV record:
# dig +short SRV _http._tcp.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# 0 50 80 <pod-hash-1>.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local.
# 0 50 80 <pod-hash-2>.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local.
# Format: <priority> <weight> <port> <target>
tasks.explanation: The `clusterIP: None` field is the only difference between the two Service manifests, but it completely changes DNS behaviour. A normal Service owns a stable virtual IP managed by kube-proxy; CoreDNS returns that one IP. A headless Service has no virtual IP, so CoreDNS synthesizes one A record per *ready* Pod endpoint instead. Clients therefore receive the actual Pod IPs and can implement their own load-balancing or sticky routing — which is exactly why StatefulSets use headless Services (each Pod has a stable DNS identity). SRV records additionally encode the port number, which allows service-mesh sidecars and GRPC load-balancers to discover both address *and* port from a single DNS query.
hints: If `dig +short db-headless...` returns only one IP instead of two, wait a few seconds — CoreDNS only includes *ready* endpoints (pods whose readiness probe passes). Check `kubectl get endpoints db-headless` to see how many addresses are registered.
The `kubectl create deployment` command does not set a label `app: db` by default — it sets `app: db-app`. Use `--labels` or patch with `kubectl label` to make the selector match.

SRV target names like `<hash>.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local` are assigned by Kubernetes for StatefulSet Pods; for ordinary Deployment Pods the format may differ slightly across Kubernetes versions. The important field to read is column 3 (port number).

tasks.title: Control DNS resolution with dnsPolicy and tune ndots for external lookups
tasks.prompt:
You have learned that `ClusterFirst` is the default and `ndots:5` causes external names to fan out across search domains before querying the real DNS. In this task you will observe both effects and apply a fix.

**Part A – dnsPolicy: Default**
1. Launch a Pod with `dnsPolicy: Default` and print its `/etc/resolv.conf`. Confirm it does *not* contain CoreDNS as the nameserver and does *not* have `cluster.local` search domains.
2. From inside that Pod, try `nslookup kubernetes` and confirm it fails (no cluster DNS).

**Part B – ndots and FQDN optimisation**
3. Launch a second Pod with `dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst` (the default) and a custom `dnsConfig` that sets `ndots: 2` instead of 5.
4. From inside, run `dig +search api.github.com +short` twice — once on the default Pod (ndots:5) to count the failing search-domain queries, and once on your ndots:2 Pod to confirm it queries `api.github.com` directly (fewer round-trips).
tasks.starter:
# Part A – Pod with dnsPolicy: Default
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: dns-default-policy
  namespace: default
spec:
  dnsPolicy: Default
  containers:
    - name: shell
      image: busybox
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

kubectl exec -it dns-default-policy -- sh
# Inside:
# cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nslookup kubernetes

# Part B – Pod with ndots:2
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: dns-ndots2
  namespace: default
spec:
  dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
  dnsConfig:
    options:
      - name: ndots
        value: "2"
  containers:
    - name: shell
      image: busybox
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

kubectl exec -it dns-ndots2 -- sh
# Inside:
# cat /etc/resolv.conf          # confirm ndots:2
# nslookup api.github.com       # should go directly, no search expansion
tasks.solution:
# Part A expected results:
#
# /etc/resolv.conf inside dns-default-policy:
#   nameserver <node-upstream-IP>   # e.g. 8.8.8.8 or the node's own resolver
#   (no cluster.local search domains, no 10.96.0.10)
#
# nslookup kubernetes (inside dns-default-policy):
#   Server:   <node-upstream-IP>
#   ** server can't find kubernetes: NXDOMAIN
#   (cluster names are not in the node's resolver, so this correctly fails)
#
# Part B expected results:
#
# /etc/resolv.conf inside dns-ndots2:
#   nameserver 10.96.0.10
#   search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
#   options ndots:2
#
# nslookup api.github.com (ndots:2 Pod):
#   api.github.com has 2 dots, which equals the threshold, so the resolver
#   tries it as a fully qualified name FIRST (no search expansion).
#   Result: direct answer from CoreDNS's upstream forwarder, one query.
#
# Contrast with ndots:5 default Pod:
#   nslookup api.github.com fires 4+ queries:
#   api.github.com.default.svc.cluster.local -> NXDOMAIN
#   api.github.com.svc.cluster.local         -> NXDOMAIN
#   api.github.com.cluster.local             -> NXDOMAIN
#   api.github.com                           -> answer

# Cleanup after the task:
# kubectl delete pod dns-default-policy dns-ndots2
tasks.explanation: **Part A** demonstrates that `dnsPolicy: Default` completely bypasses CoreDNS: the Pod uses the node's resolver, which knows nothing about `cluster.local`. This mode is correct for DaemonSet Pods that need to query external DNS before the cluster's DNS stack is healthy, but it breaks any service-to-service communication using cluster DNS names. **Part B** shows the practical consequence of `ndots:5`: a name like `api.github.com` (2 dots < 5) is treated as unqualified, so the resolver fires search-domain queries that all fail before finally querying the real name — typically 3–4 extra round-trips per external hostname. Reducing `ndots` to `2` (or writing FQDNs with a trailing dot) eliminates those round-trips. The right trade-off: lower `ndots` means fewer wasted external queries but requires more explicit naming for short in-cluster names; the default `5` optimises for cluster-internal convenience at the cost of external lookup latency.
hints: After applying the `dnsPolicy: Default` Pod, if `/etc/resolv.conf` still shows `10.96.0.10`, double-check that you spelled `dnsPolicy` correctly — a typo causes Kubernetes to silently use `ClusterFirst` as the fallback.
The `dnsConfig.options` field is a list, so `ndots` must be a string value (`"2"`, not `2`). A common mistake is omitting the quotes and getting a validation error.
To count the extra queries fired by ndots:5, run `dig +search +showsearch api.github.com` — each `SEARCH` line is a separate DNS query being attempted before the final answer.
